metadata.title: Mood Board + Style Guide: Palette, Fonts, and Rules
metadata.topic: INFO 7375 ASSIGNMENT 7 (VISUAL) — MOOD BOARD + STYLE GUIDE (E3/6)
metadata.playlist: Make It Visible — Visual Identity & Wireframes
metadata.source: INFO 7375 Assignment 7 (Visual) brief — mood board, Coolors palette, Google Fonts, style guide; due 2026-07-17

beats.narration_text: Hola — Liam, Episode 3 of 6. You have a creative brief and a selected logo. Now we build the full visual system around it — the mood board, the color palette, the font pairing, and the style guide. These four artifacts together tell any tool or collaborator exactly how the brand should look across every surface. This is the episode where nina goes from generating text to generating decisions about color and type — which means you need to evaluate the outputs against your brief and your logo, not just against aesthetics.

props.topic: INFO 7375 ASSIGNMENT 7 (VISUAL) — MOOD BOARD + STYLE GUIDE (E3/6)
props.segment: Mood Board + Style Guide: Palette, Fonts, and Rules
props.command: I have my brief and selected logo. Build the full visual system — mood board, Coolors palette, Google Fonts pairing, style guide with usage rules.
props.runningText: mood board (8–10 images, 1-sentence why) · 5-hex Coolors palette · Google Fonts pairing · style guide rules…
output: Mood board: 8–10 image categories, each with one sentence explaining the brief function served
Google Fonts: display + body (+ optional mono) · Style guide: logo rules + color combos + type hierarchy

beats.narration_text: The mood board is a reference image set — eight to ten images that show the visual energy of your brand without being your brand. It is not a Pinterest board of things you like — it is a curated set of images that each earns its place by serving a specific brief function. For each image in the mood board, you write one sentence explaining why it is there. Not 'this looks good' — 'this shows the texture I want in background treatments' or 'this demonstrates how the typography hierarchy should behave on a hero section.' Eight to ten images, eight to ten sentences. The mood board is useful because it is shareable — you can show it to anyone and ask 'does this look like a tool for cautious investors?' or 'does this feel like a community platform?' If their read matches your intent, the mood board is working. If their read is off, one or two of the images are sending the wrong signal — remove them before building the palette.

props.artifactHeading: 8–10 images. Each earns its place. Each has a brief function.
artifactLines: Not 'things I like' — images that each serve a specific brief function.
For each image: one sentence explaining why it belongs (not 'this looks good').
Example: 'shows how typography hierarchy behaves on a hero section.'
Test: show it to someone. Ask 'does this look like a tool for [your audience]?'
If their read is wrong → 1–2 images are sending the wrong signal. Remove them.
Pass this test before building the palette — the palette should match the mood board.
props.sparkLine: The mood board works when a stranger can identify your audience from the images alone.

beats.act: the Coolors palette — five hex values
beats.narration_text: The color palette is five hex values: primary, secondary, accent, background, and text. Each has a role, and each role has a rule. Primary: the brand's main color, used for headlines, primary buttons, and the logo mark. Secondary: a supporting color, used for secondary UI elements and section dividers. Accent: a single high-contrast color used sparingly for CTAs and highlights — not a color you use everywhere, a color you save for the one thing you most want the user to notice. Background: almost always white, off-white, or a light neutral — it is the canvas, not a design decision. Text: almost always near-black, not pure black — pure black at 100% opacity reads as harsh on screen. For each hex value, write a rationale sentence that connects the color back to the brief. 'Deep blue — hex 1B3A5C — institutional and trustworthy, activates the brief's financial domain signals and the calm adjective.' That sentence is what goes in the style guide.

props.artifactHeading: Each role has a rule. Each color has a rationale sentence.
artifactLines: Primary: headlines, primary buttons, logo mark.
Secondary: supporting UI elements, section dividers.
Accent: ONE high-contrast color, used sparingly — save it for the thing you most want noticed.
Background: white, off-white, or light neutral. Not a design statement.
Text: near-black (not pure black — 100% black reads harsh on screen).
For each hex: write 'Color — hex XXXXXX — [brief adjective] + [domain signal].'
props.sparkLine: The accent is the color you save, not the color you repeat. Save it for the one CTAs.

beats.act: Google Fonts pairing — display, body, mono
beats.narration_text: The font pairing is two required fonts and one optional. Display font: used for headlines and hero text — the font that carries the brand's personality. Body font: used for paragraphs and UI labels — readable at 14 to 16 pixels, not a personality font. Optional mono font: used for code, data display, or technical UI elements — if your product shows code or data to the user, you need a mono font; if it does not, skip it. All three fonts must come from Google Fonts — free, web-safe, no license issues for a student project. For the display and body pairing, test contrast: if both fonts are geometric sans-serifs with similar weights, they will fight each other on the page. The pairing works when the display font has personality and the body font has neutrality — they are doing different jobs. A display font with high personality at large sizes plus a neutral body font at small sizes is the reliable pattern.

props.artifactHeading: Display = personality. Body = neutrality. Different jobs.
artifactLines: Display font: headlines + hero text — carries the brand's personality.
Body font: paragraphs + UI labels — readable at 14–16px, not a personality font.
Mono font (optional): code or data display only. Skip if your product doesn't show data.
All fonts from Google Fonts — free, web-safe, no license issues.
Pairing test: two geometric sans-serifs of similar weight will fight. Personality + neutrality = works.
props.sparkLine: High-personality display font + neutral body font = a pairing that works at every size.

beats.narration_text: The style guide is the document that collects the logo, palette, and fonts into a single reference with usage rules. Four sections. Logo section: the primary lockup, the icon-only version, the one-color version, minimum size, clear space rules. Color section: the five swatches with hex values, RGB equivalents, and rationale sentences. Typography section: the font names and sources, the scale — H1, H2, H3, body, caption — with sizes and weights. Usage rules section: what color combinations are allowed, what is not allowed — for example, 'accent on primary is not allowed because the contrast ratio is under 4.5 to 1.' The style guide is the artifact you hand to any tool in A8 that builds the website — Vercel v0, Framer AI, or Wix all have a field where you paste the brand colors and font names. Write it now so you do not reconstruct it from memory in A8.

props.artifactHeading: The handoff document for A8's site builder
artifactLines: 1. Logo: primary lockup · icon-only · one-color version · min size · clear space.
2. Colors: 5 swatches with hex, RGB, and rationale sentences.
3. Typography: font names + scale (H1/H2/H3/body/caption) with sizes + weights.
4. Usage rules: allowed color combos + what's not allowed (with contrast ratio reason).
This is what you paste into Vercel v0, Framer AI, or Wix in A8.
Write it now — reconstructing from memory under deadline produces different colors.
props.sparkLine: The style guide is the brief a developer — or an AI site builder — acts on.

beats.narration_text: Verdict. Mood board: eight to ten images, one sentence each explaining the brief function served. Palette: five hex values — primary, secondary, accent, background, text — each with a rationale. Font pairing: display plus body from Google Fonts, optional mono. Style guide: logo, colors, typography, usage rules. The style guide is the handoff document for A8. If it is not written at the end of this episode, you will reconstruct it from memory in A8 under deadline pressure and the colors and fonts will be slightly off from what you chose here. Write it now.

props.artifactTitle: Mood Board + Style Guide: Palette, Fonts, and Rules
props.artifactHeading: The visual system in four artifacts
artifactLines: Mood board: 8–10 images, one brief-function sentence each.
Palette: 5 hex values (primary / secondary / accent / background / text) + rationale.
Font pairing: display + body from Google Fonts, optional mono.
Style guide: logo usage · color combos · typography scale · what's not allowed.
This is the A8 handoff document. Write it now, not from memory under deadline.

beats.narration_text: Your turn. Here is the nina style guide prompt. Paste it with your creative brief, selected logo concept, and logo rationale in context. The prompt reads: Here is my creative brief and selected logo: [paste brief and logo concept description]. Generate a complete visual system. Part one: a mood board brief — eight to ten image categories, each with a one-sentence brief function. Part two: a Coolors-compatible color palette — five hex values labeled primary, secondary, accent, background, text, each with a rationale sentence connecting back to the brief's adjectives. Part three: a Google Fonts pairing — display font and body font, with a note on why they work together and recommended sizes for H1, H2, H3, body, and caption. Part four: style guide usage rules — what color combinations are allowed, logo usage rules, typography hierarchy. After running the prompt: check each palette color against your mood board. If a color appears in none of the mood board images, reconsider whether it belongs in the palette.
